summary: Ronacher argues that the Rails community’s push toward OS X and GitHub reflects a closed, elitist posture rather than genuine productivity gains. He challenges the assumption that OS X is inherently superior to Windows, pointing out tradeoffs in hardware cost, editor ergonomics, and inconsistent UX. He details practical frustrations with OS X—package management gaps, confusing app bundles, Finder limitations, mouse behavior, and performance issues. He also notes OS X’s security update burden and its confusing Unix layer compared to Linux and Windows. The conclusion is that every OS has serious flaws, OS X is not a universal solution, and judging developers by their OS is lazy and ignorant.
key_points: The Rails community’s OS X evangelism is more cultural gatekeeping than a productivity mandate.
OS X’s hardware and design advantages are offset by vendor lock-in, cost, and durability issues.
Developer workflows suffer from weak package management, confusing frameworks/bundles, and inconsistent UX.
Performance, input behavior, and font rendering on OS X can be worse than Windows or Linux for developers.
No OS is a silver bullet; people should not be judged or pressured based on their platform choice.
money_quotes: OS X is just another proprietary operating system, and not the solution for all of your problems.
It tries to be Windows and Linux at the same time and fails miserably.
There are neither uninstallation tools norr do they provide a user experience that makes sense.
Windows is easy to understand, linux is not much more complicated once you groked where the stuff is located, OS X is just irritating.
And the biggest problem of all for me: It's slow. Freaking slow.
So let's conclude: every OS sucks. And OS X is no exception.
Don't judge users by their prefered operating system / desktop environment.
key_insight: OS X is not inherently better for developers; it has its own serious flaws, and OS choice should not be a proxy for competence.
